skill.name: convex-create-component
skill.description: Helps design and implement reusable Convex components with isolated tables, explicit component boundaries, app-facing wrappers, and validation steps.
skill.summary: Build reusable Convex components with isolated state, clear APIs, and safe app wrappers.
skill.author: get-convex

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many high-risk patterns, but review found they are mostly markdown, TypeScript examples, and Convex terminology false positives. The real publishable risks are that the skill directs agents to create files, run npx Convex commands, and handle environment-provided API keys in app wrapper examples.

medium_findings.title: External Convex Commands Require User Review
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run Convex codegen and dev commands, and packaged workflows may start from an npx package command. These commands are legitimate for Convex development, but they execute local or downloaded tooling and should be run only in trusted projects with user awareness.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly documented and serve a legitimate validation workflow. Risk remains because npx and project dev commands can execute package code in the user environment.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Environment Values in Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill correctly warns that component functions cannot read process.env directly, but examples show app code passing an API key into a component action. This is a valid design pattern, but it requires review to ensure components do not log, persist, or expose secrets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API key references are present, but the surrounding text frames direct component env access as bad and app-mediated access as intentional. No evidence found of exfiltration or malicious secret collection.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URL Is Benign
low_findings.description: The only hardcoded URL is an official Convex documentation link. It does not receive credentials, user data, or runtime requests from skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is visibly documentation-only and appears in reference text. No code path sends data to it.

low_findings.title: Static Markdown and Syntax Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography, SQLite, Ruby backtick, system reconnaissance, and path traversal findings are caused by markdown code fences, inline code, Convex database examples, relative TypeScript imports, or ordinary descriptive text. No evidence found of cryptographic operations, SQLite files, shell backtick execution, reconnaissance behavior, or malicious path traversal.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review matched the flagged locations to documentation prose or TypeScript examples rather than executable malicious behavior. The scanner appears to have treated markdown backticks and terms like db or ../ as dangerous by themselves.

dangerous_patterns.title: Project Tool Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends running Convex CLI commands during validation. This is expected for the skill domain, but it is still external command execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command lines are explicit and actionable. They are legitimate Convex commands, so the risk is contextual rather than evidence of malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Secret Forwarding Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows an API key being read in app code and passed to a component action. This can be safe when the component is trusted, but consumers should avoid persisting or exposing the value.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present in an example and is not hidden. The skill also says direct component env access is bad, reducing concern about malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Convex component design can be hard when tables, auth, and wrappers cross boundaries. This skill guides structure, codegen, validation, and app-facing APIs.
actual_capabilities: Chooses between local, packaged, and hybrid Convex component layouts.
Plans component-owned tables, public functions, and app wrapper responsibilities.
Creates component skeletons with convex.config.ts, schema.ts, and function files.
Explains auth, environment, HTTP route, and ID boundary rules.
Guides Convex codegen and dev validation steps for component workflows.
Provides patterns for callbacks, validators, static configuration, and client wrappers.
limitations: It assumes the project already uses Convex or is ready to add Convex.
It does not replace human review of auth, secret handling, or public APIs.
Packaged component publishing still needs project-specific build and release setup.
Validation commands can require Convex login or deployment configuration.

use_cases.title: Extract Reusable Backend Logic
use_cases.description: Move repeated Convex workflows into an isolated local component with clear app-facing wrappers.

use_cases.title: Package a Shared Component
use_cases.description: Design a component package that includes install exports, generated component types, and example app validation.

use_cases.title: Build an Integration Boundary
use_cases.description: Create a third-party integration that owns its tables while the app keeps auth, secrets, and public routes.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Local Component
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a local Convex component for notifications. First confirm that a component is justified, then plan tables, functions, wrappers, and validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Existing Logic
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Convex feature and extract reusable backend logic into a component. Keep auth and client-facing wrappers in the app.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Packaged Component
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a packaged Convex component for reuse across apps. Include package exports, codegen order, example app validation, and testing recommendations.

prompt_templates.title: Review Component Boundaries
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this Convex component design for boundary mistakes. Check table ownership, ComponentApi usage, env access, auth, HTTP routes, and validators.

output_examples.input: I need a reusable notifications component for my Convex app.
output: A recommendation to use a local component unless publishing is required.
A table and function boundary plan for notification storage and unread queries.
An app wrapper plan that keeps authentication outside the component.
A validation checklist for Convex codegen and dev mode.

output_examples.input: Can this payment integration become a packaged Convex component?
output: A package-oriented layout decision with reasons.
Required exports for installation and component typing.
Guidance to validate the built package through an example app.
Security notes for secrets, auth, and public API wrappers.

output_examples.input: Review my component for boundary problems.
output: A checklist of auth, environment, HTTP route, and ID boundary issues.
Specific recommendations for app wrappers and explicit validators.
Validation commands to run after fixing codegen and type issues.

best_practices: Confirm that isolated tables or reusable backend state justify a component before creating one.
Keep auth, environment variables, public wrappers, and HTTP route mounting in the parent app.
Run component codegen before final validation, and stop for user help when deployment setup is missing.
anti_patterns: Using a component for thin utilities that belong in normal TypeScript code.
Exposing component functions directly to clients without app-side wrappers.
Using app table ID validators inside component schemas or component arguments.

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Use it when a Convex feature needs isolated tables, reusable backend workflows, or a component boundary.

faq.question: Does it create local and packaged components?
faq.answer: Yes. It defaults to local components and switches to packaged or hybrid layouts when the goal requires them.

faq.question: Can component functions read app auth directly?
faq.answer: No. The skill keeps authentication in app wrappers and passes explicit user information across the boundary.

faq.question: How are environment variables handled?
faq.answer: The app reads environment values and passes only required values to trusted component functions.

faq.question: Does the skill run commands?
faq.answer: It recommends Convex codegen and dev commands for validation, which should be reviewed before execution.

faq.question: Is it useful for an existing Convex app?
faq.answer: Yes. It can extract existing logic into a component while preserving app-facing wrappers and validation.
